exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with modal perfect forms
exercises.title: A: Complete with modal perfect forms
exercises.instructions: Use the correct modal perfect form of the verbs in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the correct modal perfect form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: Olivia had plenty of free time, so she ...... (help) us with the decorations.
items.explanation: Past opportunity not taken → **could have helped**.

items.sentence: Jake looks terrible today. He ...... (rest) properly last night.
You ...... (warn) me about the traffic. We missed the concert!
The lights were already off, so they ...... (go) to bed early.
items.explanation: Logical deduction → **must have gone** to bed.

items.sentence: I ...... (reserve) a table earlier, but I completely forgot.
items.explanation: Missed opportunity → **could have reserved** a table.

items.sentence: Nina ...... (stay) at home yesterday because I saw her downtown.
items.explanation: Impossible (saw her downtown) → **can't have stayed** home.

items.sentence: You ...... (not / say) those rude things to your sister.
items.explanation: Wrong to say → **shouldn't have said** those things.

items.sentence: Tom ...... (leave) his notebook in the classroom by accident.

exercises.title: B: Choose the correct option
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct modal phrase in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct modal phrase in each sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: taken a taxi, but we decided to walk.
items.originalSentence: We **could have / should have** taken a taxi, but we decided to walk.
items.completedSentence: We could have taken a taxi, but we decided to walk.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "could have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "could have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "should have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.should have: "should have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("taken a taxi, but we decided to walk.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: She **might have / must have** been nervous before the interview.
items.completedSentence: She must have been nervous before the interview.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "must have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "must have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "might have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.might have: "might have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("been nervous before the interview.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: finished already — it was a huge project.
items.originalSentence: They **can't have / shouldn't have** finished already — it was a huge project.
items.completedSentence: They can't have finished already — it was a huge project.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "can't have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "can't have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "shouldn't have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.shouldn't have: "shouldn't have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("finished already — it was a huge project.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: asked permission before borrowing my laptop.
items.originalSentence: You **may have / should have** asked permission before borrowing my laptop.
items.completedSentence: You should have asked permission before borrowing my laptop.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "should have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "may have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.may have: "may have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("asked permission before borrowing my laptop.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Lucas **might have / ought to have** forgotten to lock the door.
items.completedSentence: Lucas might have forgotten to lock the door.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "might have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "might have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "ought to have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.ought to have: "ought to have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("forgotten to lock the door.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: passed the test if I had studied harder.
items.originalSentence: I **must have / could have** passed the test if I had studied harder.
items.completedSentence: I could have passed the test if I had studied harder.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "could have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "could have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "must have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.must have: "must have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("passed the test if I had studied harder.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: taken the wrong train. She's very late.
items.originalSentence: Mum **must have / should have** taken the wrong train. She's very late.
items.completedSentence: Mum must have taken the wrong train. She's very late.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "must have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "must have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "should have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.should have: "should have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("taken the wrong train. She's very late.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: You **can't have / ought to have** been kinder to your cousin.
items.completedSentence: You ought to have been kinder to your cousin.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "ought to have" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "ought to have" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "can't have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.can't have: "can't have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("been kinder to your cousin.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: C: Criticism and expectation
exercises.instructions: Complete with should have, shouldn't have or ought to have.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete with should have, shouldn't have or ought to have.

items.sentence: You ...... worn a warmer coat. It's freezing outside.
items.explanation: Should have worn warmer coat → **should have**.

items.sentence: We ...... eaten so much before the trip.
items.explanation: Shouldn't have eaten so much → **shouldn't have**.

items.sentence: I ...... paid more attention during the lesson.
They ...... laughed at him in front of everyone.
The train ...... arrived by now.
items.explanation: Expected by now → **ought to have** arrived.

items.sentence: You ...... explained the situation more clearly.
He ...... lied to his teacher.
My package ...... come yesterday.

exercises.title: D: Probability and possibility
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with must have, can't have, may have or might have.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with must have, can't have, may have or might have.

items.sentence: All the windows are open. Someone ...... forgotten to close them.
Emma isn't in her room. She ...... gone out with her friends.
items.explanation: Not in room → **might have** gone out.

items.sentence: Ryan answered every question perfectly. He ...... studied really hard.
That smell ...... come from the fridge — it's completely empty.
items.explanation: Empty fridge → smell **can't have** come from it.

items.sentence: They ...... taken the earlier bus, so they arrived before us.
items.explanation: Arrived before us → **may have** taken earlier bus.

items.sentence: This email ...... arrived this morning because I checked my inbox earlier.
items.explanation: Checked earlier → email **can't have** arrived this morning.

items.sentence: Laura looks relieved. She ...... solved the problem.
items.explanation: Looks relieved → **must have** solved the problem.

items.sentence: My wallet isn't here. I ...... dropped it at the café.

exercises.title: E: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal perfect. Find the bold word and write the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal perfect. Find the bold word and write the correct form.

items.sentence: You should spoke to the manager earlier.
items.explanation: Modal perfect + past participle → **should have spoken**.

items.sentence: They might had missed the bus.
I could have ate dinner before we left.
She can't have wrote that message.
We ought have to checked the map first.
He shouldn't have drived so fast.
It may have went wrong somehow.
You must have see that film already.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
I'm sure Ethan lost his keys. **MUST**
Ethan ...... his keys.
items.explanation: Certain past → Ethan **must have lost** his keys.

items.sentence:
It wasn't a good idea to ignore her messages. **SHOULDN'T**
You ...... her messages.
items.explanation: Bad idea → **shouldn't have ignored** her messages.

items.sentence:
Perhaps Chloe forgot her homework at home. **MIGHT**
Chloe ...... her homework at home.
items.explanation: Possibility → Chloe **might have forgotten** her homework.

items.sentence:
I had the opportunity to join the team, but I didn't. **COULD**
I ...... the team, but I didn't.
items.explanation: Didn't join → **could have joined** the team.

items.sentence:
It is impossible that Noah broke the window. **CAN'T**
Noah ...... the window.
items.explanation: Impossible → Noah **can't have broken** the window.

items.sentence:
It was wrong not to thank her. **OUGHT**
You ...... her.
items.explanation: Should have thanked → **ought to have thanked** her.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Find and correct one mistake in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Find and correct one mistake in each sentence.

items.sentence: She might have lose her phone.
He can't have went home so early.
You should have gave me a call.
They may have forget the address.
We could have done better if we had more time.
items.explanation: **Done** is already the correct past participle here.

items.sentence: I ought to have saw the warning sign.

exercises.title: H: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I'm sure he didn't steal the bicycle.
He ...... the bicycle.

It was wrong of you to interrupt the teacher.
You ...... the teacher.

I'm sure they were tired after the journey.
They ...... tired after the journey.

Perhaps she missed the beginning of the film.
She ...... the beginning of the film.

It was a pity you didn't apply for the job.
You ...... for the job.
items.explanation: Regret → **should have applied** for the job.

items.sentence:
I'm sure the exam wasn't easy.
It ...... easy.
